_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Todd County, Kentucky. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the birthplace of Jefferson Davis — formed 1820 from Christian and Logan counties, with Elkton as county seat. Todd County's records are well-preserved; no major courthouse fires or war damage. The county's small size and rural character make church records and agricultural census schedules particularly important substitute sources.

previous_courthouses.location: Elkton — original log and frame courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county matured. Records transferred to successor building.

previous_courthouses.location: Elkton — brick courthouse on the same site
previous_courthouses.fate: Served nearly a century. No major fire or war damage. Replaced by the current 1951 building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Todd County's courthouse has not experienced a significant fire or destructive event. The Civil War brought guerrilla activity and disruption to the county, but the courthouse was not damaged. Records run continuously from the county's 1820 formation. This makes Todd County one of the best-preserved small counties in the cluster. Gaps in the record are more likely to reflect the family's circumstances — poverty, tenancy, or pre-1820 residence in the parent counties — than courthouse destruction.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Todd County after 1820, the courthouse records almost certainly survive. Gaps in the paper trail point to the ancestor's economic circumstances — tenant farmers who owned no land and poor families who left no estates appear only in census records and church minutes.
record_losses.source: Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Kentucky County Records. Frankfort, 1937-1942.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Todd County is small and its deed books are manageable and well-maintained.
deeds.repository: Todd County Clerk, Elkton; KDLA microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Kentucky land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — will books survive from county formation
wills_estates.gaps: Minor — some loose estate papers from the earliest decades are incomplete. Bound volumes are intact.

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation
marriages.gaps: Marriage bonds are more complete than minister returns before the 1850s.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — county court order books are continuous from formation
court_minutes.gaps: No significant gaps. The court order books document the plantation society that dominated antebellum Todd County.

vital_records.completeness: Kentucky mandated registration in 1852, but compliance in rural Todd County was minimal until approximately 1911.
vital_records.repository: Kentucky Office of Vital Statistics, Frankfort; KDLA for pre-1911 registers

substitute_sources.source_type: Christian and Logan County parent records (pre-1820)
substitute_sources.description: Todd County was formed from Christian and Logan counties in 1820. All records before that date are in the parent counties. Logan's 1865 fire destroyed some early records, making Christian County's well-preserved records the more reliable substitute for pre-1820 Todd County families.
substitute_sources.repository: Christian County Clerk, Hopkinsville; Logan County Clerk, Russellville; KDLA microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any family in the Elkton area before 1820. Christian County records (intact from 1797) are the primary substitute source given Logan County's 1865 fire losses.
substitute_sources.source: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1820-1940. Slave schedules (1850-1860) and agricultural schedules (1850-1880). Todd County is enumerated from the 1820 census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Todd County had a significant enslaved population relative to its small size — the slave schedules are important. The agricultural schedules document the tobacco and livestock economy that defined the county.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Todd County, KY.

substitute_sources.source_type: Jefferson Davis birthplace and related records
substitute_sources.description: Jefferson Davis was born at Fairview, Todd County, in 1808 (when the area was still part of Christian County). The Jefferson Davis State Historic Site and associated genealogical collections include records of the Davis family and neighboring families.
substitute_sources.repository: Jefferson Davis State Historic Site, Fairview; Kentucky Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Niche but useful — families who were neighbors of the Davis family in the Fairview area may appear in Davis-related correspondence and property records.
substitute_sources.source: Cooper, William J., Jr. Jefferson Davis, American. New York: Alfred A. Knopf, 2000.

narrative_hooks: Todd County's courthouse has never burned — {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court records survive intact from 1820 forward. If the records are missing, the question is not what the courthouse lost but what the family never owned.

Before Todd County existed in 1820, {ancestor_name}'s records were filed in Christian County at Hopkinsville or Logan County at Russellville — and the Logan fire of 1865 makes the Christian County records the more reliable trail.

Todd County's small, rural character means the census is often the only record of tenant families — {ancestor_name} may appear in the federal census and nowhere else if {pronoun_subject} never owned land or left an estate.

Jefferson Davis was born in Todd County in 1808, and the families who lived alongside the Davis clan on the Fairview road left traces in the same deed books and court orders — {ancestor_name}'s neighbors included the family that would produce the president of the Confederacy.
source_references: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.
Cooper, William J., Jr. Jefferson Davis, American. New York: Alfred A. Knopf, 2000.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Kentucky County Records. Frankfort, 1937-1942.
Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Records on Microfilm. Frankfort, various editions.
